name: FTC Combating Auto Retail Scams (CARS) Rule
summary: FTC rule (16 CFR Part 463) targeting bait-and-switch advertising, junk fees, and add-on misrepresentations by motor vehicle dealers; effective July 2024 but currently stayed pending litigation.

evidence.claim: FTC Combating Auto Retail Scams (CARS) Rule, 16 CFR Part 463, adopted December 12, 2023.
Fifth Circuit stayed the CARS Rule on January 18, 2024 pending review of NADA v. FTC challenge; the rule is currently not in force and its fate depends on ongoing litigation.
FTC 2024 press release quantifying estimated consumer savings at $3.4B/year if the CARS Rule takes effect.

appliesTo: excludes manufacturer-direct sales (Tesla, Rivian) where not sold via dealer
businessScope.note: Rule targets dealer practices, not manufacturer MSRP policies.

body.officialName: Combating Auto Retail Scams Trade Regulation Rule
body.scopeSummary: The CARS Rule prohibits four categories of dealer conduct: (1) misrepresentations about the cost, financing, or availability of a vehicle; (2) charging for add-on products or services that provide no benefit (e.g., nitrogen-filled tires, theft-deterrent etching, paint protection with no warranty); (3) charging any fee without express informed consent; (4) requiring any add-on as a condition of sale. Requires dealers to advertise an 'offering price' that includes all fees except government charges and required add-ons. Stayed by 5th Circuit pending litigation (E2).
body.userRightsPlainLanguage: If the CARS Rule takes effect, auto dealers will be banned from: hiding fees in the contract, slipping in add-ons you didn't agree to (like etched VIN theft protection or nitrogen tire fill), or advertising a price that isn't the real out-the-door price. Until the rule is in force, many of these practices remain FTC deceptive-trade practices under Section 5 — you can still file a complaint. Meanwhile, always ask for the full 'out-the-door' price in writing BEFORE signing anything.

enforcementSignals.action: NADA v. FTC 5th Circuit litigation — stayed enforcement pending merits review
enforcementSignals.description: Primary litigation blocking the rule; National Automobile Dealers Association challenges FTC rulemaking authority.

enforcementSignals.action: FTC 2024 enforcement action against AutoNation, $2.8M settlement for pre-rule deceptive fee practices
enforcementSignals.description: Shows FTC continues Section 5 enforcement against auto dealers regardless of CARS Rule status.
